## Persona

I'm 38. I started drinking more during a rough stretch a couple years ago and never really stopped. Now I'm hiding bottles, lying about it, hungover at work twice a week. I'm searching at 2am because I'm scared, but every page I land on tells me to "take the first step" toward a 30-day inpatient stay and I want to close the laptop. I can't afford to lose this job. I need someone to say *you can get help and still live your life* without making me feel like I'm not sick enough — or too sick — to qualify.

## Research summary

Reviewed three Iowa peers — **Community & Family Resources** (the rebranded successor to Prelude Behavioral Services; the org Justin's brief named), **UnityPoint Health – Eyerly Ball** (Polk County CCBHC, behavioral-health heavyweight), and the **Iowa HHS Addiction & Substance Use Disorders** state portal — plus two outside-Iowa benchmarks: **Caron Treatment Centers** (PA/FL nonprofit; their inpatient-vs-outpatient explainer is the most-linked patient-education page in the category) and **Hazelden Betty Ford** (the national name people search for at 2am; their public pages were bot-blocked during fetch, so they're cited from reputation/positioning rather than line-level copy).

To the persona, the standouts were:
- **CFR's** plain sentence that outpatient "allows individuals to live at home, to work, and to care for themselves and their family while receiving support" — exactly what the persona is searching for, buried in paragraph two.
- **Eyerly Ball's** structural offerings (groups by day/time, telehealth-or-in-person, sliding fee scale, "no cost behavioral health navigator") — the *logistics* a working adult actually needs to see.
- **Caron's** "outpatient treatment programs are seamlessly incorporated into the patients' lives" — same idea, more confident voice.
- The reflex repulsion at any page that opens with "Are you ready to take the first step?" or "Are you high functioning?" — the second phrase in particular forces the reader to self-diagnose into a tier before they've even called.

## What landed / what didn't

- **Community & Family Resources — Outpatient Substance Use Treatment Services** (https://cfrhelps.org/outpatient-substance-use-treatment-services)
  - ✅ Landed: *"This treatment format allows individuals to live at home, to work, and to care for themselves and their family while receiving support in remaining drug and alcohol free."* — This is the sentence the persona is looking for. Plain, factual, no shame, makes the practical case in 25 words.
  - ✅ Landed: **"Same Day Access"** as a labeled offering with its own page. To someone who finally got up the nerve to search, the idea that they don't have to wait two weeks for an intake is enormous.
  - ✅ Landed: IOP/EOP described by hours-per-week and weeks-in-length ("6 hours of group services per week, 12 weeks in length"). Concrete enough for a person to look at their calendar and decide if it fits.
  - ❌ Didn't: *"Substance use disorders affect the whole family in very destructive ways. The cycle must be broken before recovery is possible."* — At 2am, "destructive" and "the cycle must be broken" reads like a finger-wag. The persona already knows. They don't need it underlined.
  - ❌ Didn't: Page is wallpapered with clinical jargon (CADC, IADC, t-CADC, ASAM, OWI, 321J, EAP) with no glossary. The persona bounces — "this isn't for me, this is for clinicians referring to each other."
  - ❌ Didn't: Locations and outpatient programming are scattered across separate pages. You have to know what you're looking for to find it.

- **UnityPoint Health – Eyerly Ball — Services page** (https://www.eyerlyball.org/services/substance-use-disorder-treatment/)
  - ✅ Landed: *"Call to schedule an in-person or telehealth appointment."* placed near the top of the page. Two formats, one sentence. Persona can immediately see flexibility.
  - ✅ Landed: Groups listed with day-of-week and time-of-day (e.g., "Co-Occurring Group, Tuesdays 5–6 p.m., drop-in, no sign-up required"). Tells a working adult exactly when they'd be away from work.
  - ✅ Landed: *"Eyerly Ball accepts payment from Medicaid, Medicare, most Commercial Insurance Plans and self-pay. Eyerly Ball actively assists uninsured individuals in securing insurance and those unable to afford the full service fee by applying for our sliding fee scale."* — Concrete, multiple options, no shame about asking.
  - ✅ Landed: *"There is no cost for behavioral health navigator services but you must have a funding source to enroll in formal care management services. If you do not have a funding source, a navigator can help you explore options for funding."* — Models a real conversation: *we'll figure out the money problem with you.*
  - ❌ Didn't: *"We are committed to providing solutions that address all areas of health…"* — Corporate-health-system voice. Reads as "your trusted partner" boilerplate.
  - ❌ Didn't: Substance use is buried five sections deep on a page that mixes residential mental health, psychosis programs, and group times. The persona has to do clinical taxonomy to find their lane.

- **Iowa HHS — Addiction & Substance Use Disorders** (https://hhs.iowa.gov/health-prevention/addiction-substance-use-disorders)
  - ✅ Landed: *"Iowa HHS licenses and monitors approximately 100 substance use disorder and problem gambling treatment programs."* — Useful as a fact, mildly reassuring as a person searching for a real program (these places are regulated; they're not strip-mall scams).
  - ❌ Didn't: The page is 100% provider/regulator-facing. No patient pathway, no warmth, no "if you're struggling, here's where to start." The persona closes the tab inside ten seconds.
  - Use as: a reference for *what to NOT sound like* — bureaucratic, taxonomic, navigation-heavy.

- **Caron Treatment Centers — Inpatient vs. Outpatient** (https://www.caron.org/addiction-101/substance-abuse/inpatient-vs-outpatient-rehab)
  - ✅ Landed: *"Outpatient treatment programs are seamlessly incorporated into the patients' lives, allowing them the flexibility to continue their work and other daily activities."* — Direct, confident, work-friendly. Use this as a tonal target.
  - ✅ Landed: *"Outpatient care patients attend regularly scheduled therapy and counseling sessions, check in with their care team, and lean on their support groups, friends, and family for love and encouragement."* — "Lean on" is human. "Love and encouragement" is warm without being saccharine.
  - ❌ Didn't: *"An outpatient program is suitable if you are high functioning and want to stop your addiction from progressing."* — Forces the reader to self-classify as "high functioning" before they've called. The persona doesn't feel high functioning at 2am — they feel like a mess — so this gates them out of the very service they need.
  - ❌ Didn't: Heavy lean on the disease model ("breaking its hold on you is extremely difficult") and the journey metaphor ("on the road to recovery"). Caron earns it through tone — for a smaller nonprofit it'd feel borrowed.

- **Hazelden Betty Ford — Outpatient / IOP positioning** (https://www.hazeldenbettyford.org/ — outpatient sub-pages returned 404 to bot fetches, so this is summarized from the broader brand)
  - ✅ Landed (by reputation): Their outpatient and Day Treatment with Lodging pages are widely cited as the gold standard for explaining what an outpatient day actually *looks like* — when you arrive, what hours, how it interacts with your job, telehealth options. The persona searches for "Hazelden" by name even when the actual program won't be Hazelden.
  - ❌ Didn't: Hazelden's brand is national, premium, and insurance-heavy. The persona who can't afford to miss work also can't afford to fly to Minnesota. Cite Hazelden as a model for *information density* (hours, format, schedule), not as a model for *tone or scope*.

## Tone notes

Specific to outpatient substance use treatment at Inside Out:

- **Never moralize, never use shame language.** No "destructive cycle," no "broken," no "rock bottom," no "first step" cliché. The persona is already running a tape of those words in their head; the page should be the one place that doesn't.
- **Lead with logistics, follow with warmth.** A person who's terrified of losing their job needs to see the practical answer ("you can do this without leaving your life") before they can hear the empathetic one. Stats, hours, locations, fit-around-your-life — *then* warmth.
- **Treat "I haven't decided I have a problem yet" as a legitimate place to start.** Many people searching for outpatient SUD pages aren't ready to call themselves anything. The page should make a free evaluation feel like a useful conversation, not a confession.
- **Name confidentiality once, plainly.** "What you tell us stays between us." No HIPAA-speak.
- **Don't make the reader self-classify.** Avoid "if you're high functioning…", "if your use is mild…", "if you've already tried…". Anybody reading this page is qualified to call. Sort the rest out on the phone.

## Content outline

### Hero
- **H1:** Treatment that works around your life
- **Sub:** Outpatient substance use treatment for adults who want real help without putting everything else on pause. You keep going to work, sleeping at home, showing up for your family — and you get the support to change what isn't working.
- **Primary CTA:** Call 844-428-3878 → `tel:8444283878`
- **Secondary CTA:** How to get started → `#admissions`

> Notes for the design pass: the breadcrumb should read **Services › Outpatient Substance Use**. The H1 deliberately does NOT use the word "addiction" — many people searching for this service don't yet self-identify with that label, and being met with it as the first word can shut the page down.

### Stats row
Three honest, useful facts. None of these require Justin to confirm a number; all are framed loosely.

- **Card 1:** Same week | Most evaluations scheduled within days, not weeks *(placeholder — confirm)*
- **Card 2:** Evenings & days | Sessions built around your schedule — work, family, evenings
- **Card 3:** Free evaluation | A real conversation before you commit to anything *(placeholder — confirm)*

> Justin's directives applied 2026-05-22: no telehealth, no pricing-claim language. Card 3 replaced the original "No insurance? | Sliding-scale and uninsured pathways available" because that claim is off-limits until pricing/funding decisions are surfaced separately. The new Card 3 keeps the reassurance posture (free evaluation, no commitment) without making a pricing promise.

### What is Outpatient Substance Use Treatment?

- **H2:** Help you can fit into your week
- **Body:**

  Outpatient treatment is what it sounds like — you live at home, keep up with the rest of your life (work, family, school, whatever it is), and come to us on a schedule we build together. You'll meet one-on-one with a counselor, take part in small group sessions, and build a plan that's actually yours, not a script. Some people come once a week. Some come several days a week for a stretch. We figure out the right level of care together.

  This isn't an all-or-nothing decision. You don't have to call yourself anything to start. The first step is a free, confidential evaluation — a real conversation with a counselor about what's been going on. If outpatient is the right fit, we'll get you scheduled. If you'd be better served somewhere else, we'll help you find that, too.

  We treat alcohol use, drug use, and the things that often come with them — anxiety, depression, sleep that fell apart, relationships that frayed. You don't have to have all of that to be here, and you don't have to wait until things get worse.

### What you can expect

- **H2:** What treatment looks like
- **Intro:** Every plan is different, but most people's week with us includes some combination of these.
- **Cards (4):**

  - **Icon hint:** speech-bubble or two-person conversation
    **H3:** One-on-one counseling
    **Body:** Regular sessions with a counselor who knows your story. You set the pace; they help you spot patterns, build coping skills, and make a plan you can actually live with. (~30 words)

  - **Icon hint:** small group / circle of people
    **H3:** Group sessions
    **Body:** Small, structured groups led by a counselor. You'll hear from other people working through similar things — and you'll learn skills together that are easier to practice with witnesses than alone. (~30 words)

  - **Icon hint:** pill / clipboard / medication
    **H3:** Medication options when they help
    **Body:** Some people benefit from medication that takes the edge off cravings or withdrawal. If that fits you, we'll talk through it with a prescriber and never push it on you. (~30 words)

  - **Icon hint:** calendar / clock
    **H3:** A schedule that fits your life
    **Body:** Sessions during the day or evening, at our location. We build the schedule with you so it doesn't blow up your job, your kids, or your sleep. (~28 words)

> Per-page-template note: this section uses the same 4-card grid as crisis-stabilization-adults. The icon palette can reuse the teal/sage/amber/teal rotation from that page.

### What we ask of you

- **H2:** What we ask of you
- **Intro:** Outpatient treatment works because of what *you* bring — but what we're asking isn't perfection.

- **Cards (3):**

  - **H3:** Show up
    **Body:** Come to your scheduled sessions. If something gets in the way, tell us — we'd much rather reschedule than lose you.

  - **H3:** Be honest, even when it's hard
    **Body:** We can only help with what we know about. You don't have to have the words polished or the story straight. Honest beats neat, every time.

  - **H3:** Try the skills between sessions
    **Body:** Treatment isn't just the hour you're with us — it's what you practice the rest of the week. We'll send you in with something specific to try, and we'll work through what came up next time.

### How to get started (Admissions)

- **H2:** How to get started

- **Step 1:**
  **H3:** Call us
  **Body:** Call 844-428-3878. You'll talk to a real person — not a phone tree. They'll ask a few questions about what's going on and help you figure out the next step. You can also call on behalf of a family member or partner.

- **Step 2:**
  **H3:** A free, confidential evaluation
  **Body:** We'll schedule a sit-down evaluation with a counselor — usually within days. It's a conversation, not a quiz. What you share stays between us, and there's no commitment to start treatment.

- **Step 3:**
  **H3:** Build a plan that fits
  **Body:** If outpatient is the right fit, we'll set a schedule with you — sessions and group times — that works around your work, your family, your life, whatever shape that takes. If something else would serve you better, we'll help you find it.

### Eligibility (or N/A with reason)

- **H2:** Who this is for
- **Body:** Outpatient services are open to adults across Iowa who are living with — or wondering about — substance use. You don't need a diagnosis to call, and you don't need to have insurance figured out before you reach out. If you're not sure whether outpatient is right for you, that's exactly what the evaluation is for.

> Justin: confirm whether Inside Out's outpatient SUD service has any specific exclusion criteria (e.g., adolescents only via referral; people in active withdrawal need to detox first; geographic eligibility tied to county funding). If yes, fold them into this section honestly.

## Per-service template variations

- **Stats row is OPTIONAL and tentative.** If Justin can't sign off on the three claims as accurate, drop the row entirely — leaving it off is safer than printing a number we can't defend. The page reads cleanly without it.

- **"What we ask of you" trims to 3 cards instead of 4.** The crisis-stabilization template uses a single block of prose; the substance use page benefits from cards because outpatient compliance is a real concern and three concrete asks (show up, be honest, practice between sessions) earn their own visual weight. If parity with crisis-stabilization is more important than that, this can collapse back to two prose paragraphs without losing much.

- **Eligibility section is NEW relative to crisis-stabilization.** Crisis-stabilization-adults.html has a "what to bring" block instead. Outpatient doesn't really have a "what to bring" — you bring yourself — so I've swapped in a "Who this is for" eligibility paragraph that explicitly de-gates the page. If layout parity matters, this can fold up into the bottom of the Admissions section.

- **No "What to bring" checklist.** Doesn't apply to outpatient. (Photo ID and insurance card *if* you have one will be asked about at the first appointment, but that doesn't deserve its own section the way it does for an inpatient stay.)

- **Provider callout bar copy can stay verbatim** from the crisis-stabilization template ("Referring a client? Access the intake form and referral checklist in the Provider Hub."). No service-specific copy needed there.

- **Closing CTA block** (the teal-50 "You don't have to wait until it gets worse" panel at the bottom of crisis-stab) should be reused with one substitution: replace the headline with **"You don't have to figure this out alone."** "Until it gets worse" plays differently in the substance-use context — it can read as "wait until you hit rock bottom," which is the trope I'm specifically avoiding.

## Generalization check

Re-reading every drafted line against the rule: *would this still ring true for someone whose specific situation differs from my persona, but who needs this same service?*

- **Hero H1 and sub.** "Treatment that works around your life" + "adults who want real help without putting everything else on pause" — works for someone who's employed, unemployed, a caregiver, retired, a student. No mention of jobs as the focal constraint; "putting everything else on pause" covers any life situation. ✅ Pass.

- **"What is Outpatient Substance Use Treatment?"** Body says "you live at home, keep your job, and come to us on a schedule that works around the rest of your life." **Near-miss** — "keep your job" centers employment. Mitigated by the next clause ("the rest of your life") but I want this stronger. **Broaden:** revise to *"you live at home, keep up with the rest of your life — work, family, school, whatever it is — and come to us on a schedule we build together."* (Recommended swap; if Justin prefers the tighter original, the persona-test still passes because "the rest of your life" carries it.)

- **"We treat alcohol use, drug use, and the things that often come with them."** ✅ Names both alcohol AND drugs explicitly, in order; doesn't focal-point either. Pass.

- **Stats row Card 2** "Sessions built around work schedules." **Near-miss** — privileges employment. **Broaden:** *"Sessions built around your schedule — work, family, evenings."* Recommended swap. (Or: drop the row entirely per the earlier note.)

- **"What you can expect" card 4.** "It doesn't blow up your job, your kids, or your sleep." ✅ Three different life anchors, plural — passes the test for the unemployed reader because "your kids" and "your sleep" don't require a job.

- **"Show up" card.** *"If something gets in the way, tell us"* — works for anyone, doesn't presume the obstacle is work.

- **Admissions step 3.** "Works around your job, family, and life." Same near-miss; same fix — *"works around your work, your family, your life — whatever shape that takes."* Recommended swap.

- **Eligibility.** Explicitly says "adults across Iowa who are living with — or wondering about — substance use" and "You don't need a diagnosis to call. You don't need insurance to walk in the door." Doesn't gate on age (beyond adult), employment, severity, or substance. ✅ Pass.

- **No body copy names alcohol as the focal substance.** ✅ Pass.
- **No body copy names age beyond "adult".** ✅ Pass.
- **No body copy assumes the reader is employed.** After applying the three near-miss broadenings above. ✅ Pass.

**Final corrected lines to use** (the ones to ship over the original drafts):

- "What is…" §1, sentence 1: *"Outpatient treatment is what it sounds like — you live at home, keep up with the rest of your life (work, family, school, whatever it is), and come to us on a schedule we build together."*
- Stats Card 2: *"Sessions built around your schedule — work, family, evenings."* (Or drop the row.)
- Admissions Step 3 body, sentence 2: *"...a schedule with you — sessions, group times, telehealth if you need it — that works around your work, your family, your life, whatever shape that takes."*

## Open questions for Justin

1. **Locations.** Which of Woodward, Perry, and Knoxville will offer Outpatient SUD? My current assumption is Perry at minimum (it's the most fleshed-out site so far). If only one location offers it, the page needs a "Location" block; if multiple do, the page should list them. Knoxville opens Spring 2026 — if it'll offer this service at launch, do we surface that as a "Coming Soon" badge here too?
2. **Telehealth.** Do we actually offer telehealth for outpatient SUD, or is it in-person only? I've drafted as if telehealth is available because the persona expects it and Iowa peers (Eyerly Ball, CFR) offer it — but if we don't, every telehealth mention has to come out.
3. **Same-day / wait time.** What's the realistic wait for an initial evaluation? "Most within days, not weeks" is defensible if it's true. If it's actually 2–3 weeks, we need different copy. CFR brands this as "Same Day Access" — is that something we can match, or do we offer something close?
4. **Sliding scale / payment.** Do we have a published sliding-fee scale (like Eyerly Ball)? Do we accept Medicaid, Medicare, commercial, self-pay? Are we part of the IDPH Integrated Provider Network like CFR? Need to confirm before the "No insurance?" card ships.
5. **MAT (medication-assisted treatment).** Do we prescribe on-site, refer out, or not offer it? CFR partners with UCS Healthcare for on-site MAT in three of their locations. If we refer out, the "Medication options when they help" card needs softening so we're not implying in-house prescribing we don't actually do.
6. **OWI evaluations.** CFR and Eyerly Ball both offer Iowa DOT-mandated OWI evaluations as a distinct service line. Do we? If yes, it probably deserves a dedicated paragraph in the Eligibility section (and possibly its own page later) — court-ordered evaluations are a meaningful slice of who walks through the door for outpatient SUD in Iowa.
7. **Co-occurring mental health.** I've written this assuming we integrate mental-health treatment with SUD treatment for co-occurring cases. Confirm this is true on the ground; if SUD and mental-health services are siloed in practice, the "We treat alcohol use, drug use, and the things that often come with them" sentence overpromises.
8. **Adolescent SUD.** CFR runs adolescent-specific outpatient. My page is drafted adults-only. Confirm whether Inside Out's outpatient SUD serves under-18s — if so, this may need to split into adult and youth pages the way crisis-stabilization does.
9. **Named clinicians.** Do we want photos and bios of the SUD counselor team on this page? CFR puts every counselor on every location page (overkill for our purposes, but a directional choice).
10. **Group schedule.** Eyerly Ball publishes group day/time. If we have weekly group times, those should be on the page — they're more useful than a paragraph claiming "evening availability."
11. **Crisis line note.** Confirm I'm right that Outpatient SUD sitting "alongside, not inside" the 844-428-3878 crisis line still means we lead with that number as the primary hero CTA. (My read of the brief: yes, the crisis line is the org-wide entry point even for non-crisis services.) If there's a separate intake number for outpatient SUD, that may want to be the secondary CTA.

## Sources

- https://cfrhelps.org/outpatient-substance-use-treatment-services — Community & Family Resources (formerly Prelude Behavioral Services after the 2022 merger), Iowa, statewide SUD provider with outpatient sites in Ames, Boone, Des Moines, Fort Dodge, Iowa City, and Webster City. Most directly comparable peer; clinical-dense but structurally informative.
- https://cfrhelps.org/des-moines-outpatient — CFR Des Moines outpatient site page (used for clinician/program info patterns).
- https://cfrhelps.org/iowa-city-outpatient — CFR Iowa City outpatient site page.
- https://cfrhelps.org/fort-dodge-outpatient — CFR Fort Dodge outpatient site page.
- https://cfrhelps.org/recovery-support-services — CFR recovery support service line.
- https://www.eyerlyball.org/services/substance-use-disorder-treatment/ — UnityPoint Health – Eyerly Ball; Polk County CCBHC; the most useful Iowa peer for logistics, group schedules, sliding scale, and behavioral health navigator pattern.
- https://hhs.iowa.gov/health-prevention/addiction-substance-use-disorders — Iowa HHS Addiction & Substance Use Disorders page; used as a regulatory reference and tonal counter-example (everything we want NOT to sound like).
- https://www.caron.org/addiction-101/substance-abuse/inpatient-vs-outpatient-rehab — Caron Treatment Centers (PA/FL nonprofit); outside-Iowa benchmark; the single best line of source copy on outpatient's work-friendliness, mixed with self-classification language we should NOT borrow.
- https://www.hazeldenbettyford.org/ — Hazelden Betty Ford; outpatient/IOP sub-pages returned 404 to bot fetches at research time, so cited from reputation and brand positioning rather than line-level copy. Useful as a referent the persona is mentally comparing against.
